source_text: Section 9.1 SUBCONTRACTORS: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors and for compliance by such subcontractors with the applicable terms of this Agreement. Lattice is not required to provide advance notice to Customer of, or obtain Customer's consent to, the engagement, replacement, or termination of any subcontractor.' + Section 5.1 CUSTOMER DATA LICENSE: 'Customer hereby grants Lattice a non-exclusive, worldwide, royalty-free license to host, copy, transmit, display, and otherwise process Customer Data solely as necessary for Lattice to provide and support the Subscription Services' + PROFILE REQUIREMENT: 'Notice required + customer approval for material subcontractors (defined as those processing customer data or providing >25% of services)'
external_comment: The Agreement grants Lattice unlimited subcontracting rights for all obligations including processing of Customer Data, with no notice or consent requirement. This creates supply-chain visibility and control gaps that prevent us from satisfying our own compliance obligations to downstream customers. We need advance notice and approval rights for any subcontractor who will process Customer Data or provide more than 25% of the Services, with mandatory flowdown of the data security and confidentiality obligations in Sections 5 and 7. This is essential for our vendor management and due diligence obligations, particularly given that you handle our operational data and we have downstream commitments to our own customers regarding data-handling practices.
materiality_rationale: Customer is in software/SaaS and likely has contractual obligations to its own customers regarding data handling, typically requiring Customer to vet and approve third parties who access customer data. Lattice's unilateral right to subcontract data processing without notice eliminates Customer's ability to perform supply-chain due diligence, creates undetectable risk if subcontractors lack adequate security controls, and may place Customer in breach of its own downstream obligations. The Profile explicitly requires notice and approval for material subcontractors processing customer data.
position: Amend Section 9.1 to require: (a) Lattice to provide written notice to Customer at least thirty (30) days in advance of engaging any subcontractor who will process Customer Data or provide more than 25% of the Subscription Services by volume or criticality; (b) Customer to have the right to review the subcontractor's security and privacy practices and to withhold consent if the subcontractor does not meet Customer's reasonable security standards (consistent with Section 5.2's SOC 2 requirement); (c) Lattice to include in all subcontracts flowdown provisions requiring subcontractors to comply with Sections 5 (Customer Data; Data Security; Privacy), 7 (Confidentiality), and any applicable Data Processing Addendum; and (d) Lattice to remain primarily liable for subcontractor performance and breaches.
fallback: Notice required for all subcontractors processing Customer Data, with consent required only for subcontractors located outside the United States or processing more than 50% of Customer Data by volume. Flowdown of Sections 5 and 7 mandatory for all subcontractors processing Customer Data.
walkaway: If Lattice refuses any notice or consent requirement for subcontractors processing Customer Data, and refuses to commit to flowdown of data-security and confidentiality obligations, Customer should walk.

source_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of three (3) years thereafter
proposed_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter
external_comment: Three years is short relative to the typical lifecycle of competitive and operational information in SaaS relationships. Five years is more appropriate for information that retains competitive value.
materiality_rationale: Customer's operational data, integration patterns, and business processes disclosed to Lattice retain competitive value beyond three years. A three-year tail exposes Customer to earlier-than-appropriate disclosure or use by Lattice in competitive contexts.
position: Five (5) year survival period for confidentiality obligations relating to non-trade-secret Confidential Information.
fallback: Four (4) years if Lattice objects to five.
suggested_text: The obligations of confidentiality set forth in this Section 7 will continue during the Subscription Term and for a period of five (5) years thereafter

category: IP ownership — Customer-specific outputs and analytics
proposed_text: 6.4 Customer-Specific Outputs. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other outputs that are specifically developed for Customer or derived exclusively from Customer Data ('Customer-Specific Outputs') shall be jointly owned by the Parties. Each Party shall have the right to use Customer-Specific Outputs without restriction, provided that Lattice may not disclose Customer-Specific Outputs to third parties in a manner that identifies Customer or reveals Customer's Confidential Information without Customer's prior written consent.
external_comment: The current IP allocation gives Lattice full ownership of all analytics and outputs, including those derived entirely from our operational data. We should retain ownership or at least joint ownership of intelligence and insights specific to our business.
materiality_rationale: Customer-specific analytics, optimization recommendations, and operational intelligence derived from Customer Data represent competitive value to Customer. Under the current language, Lattice owns these outputs outright and could potentially use them to benefit Customer's competitors or in developing competing products. This misalignment creates concrete competitive exposure.
position: Customer owns or jointly owns all Customer-specific outputs, reports, and analytics derived from Customer Data.
fallback: Lattice may use Customer-specific outputs internally and in aggregated/anonymized form, but may not disclose them to third parties in identifiable form or use them to directly benefit Customer's competitors.
suggested_text: 6.4 Customer-Specific Outputs. Notwithstanding Section 6.1, any reports, dashboards, configurations, analytics, or other outputs that are specifically developed for Customer or derived exclusively from Customer Data ('Customer-Specific Outputs') shall be jointly owned by the Parties. Each Party shall have the right to use Customer-Specific Outputs without restriction, provided that Lattice may not disclose Customer-Specific Outputs to third parties in a manner that identifies Customer or reveals Customer's Confidential Information without Customer's prior written consent.

proposed_text: Customer and its Affiliates shall be named as additional insureds on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services under this Agreement.
external_comment: To ensure we have direct recourse under Lattice's E&O policy for covered claims, we request additional-insured status on the E&O policy. This is standard for SaaS engagements where the customer is exposed to provider errors in service delivery.
materiality_rationale: Without additional-insured status, Customer must rely on Lattice's financial capacity to satisfy judgments or settlements in E&O claims. Additional-insured status gives Customer direct access to the $5M E&O policy limits, reducing collection risk and accelerating claim resolution. In a mid-market SaaS deal with operational data in scope, this is material protection.
position: Customer named as additional insured on Lattice's E&O/cyber policy for claims arising from Lattice's services under this Agreement.
fallback: Customer named as additional insured on E&O policy for claims arising solely from Lattice's negligence in provision of Subscription Services.
jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (governing law per Section 10.1); additional-insured endorsements are governed by insurance policy terms and do not vary materially by U.S. jurisdiction for commercial E&O policies.
suggested_text: Customer and its Affiliates shall be named as additional insureds on Lattice's errors and omissions / professional liability insurance policy with respect to claims arising out of Lattice's provision of the Subscription Services under this Agreement.

source_text: Lattice may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Customer if Customer materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail. Customer may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to Lattice if Lattice materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within sixty (60) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
proposed_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.
external_comment: Section 4.3 grants Lattice a 30-day cure period but requires us to wait 60 days before terminating for Lattice's breach. Symmetric cure periods are standard in commercial agreements of this type and ensure parity in remedies. We propose equalizing the cure period at 30 days for both Parties.
materiality_rationale: The asymmetric cure period favors Lattice and limits Customer's ability to terminate for Lattice's breach. If Lattice suffers a Security Incident and fails to remediate within 30 days, Customer must wait an additional 30 days (60 total) before terminating, during which period the Security Incident may continue to expose Customer Data. In contrast, Lattice can terminate for Customer's payment breach after only 30 days. This imbalance creates real exposure in time-sensitive breach scenarios such as data security failures or sustained SLA failures.
position: Equalize cure period at 30 days for both Parties.
fallback: Equalize cure period at 45 days for both Parties.
suggested_text: Either Party may terminate this Agreement or any Order Form upon written notice to the other Party if such other Party materially breaches this Agreement and fails to cure such breach within thirty (30) days after receipt of written notice describing the breach in reasonable detail.

proposed_text: 2.5(a) Deliverables Ownership. Any deliverables, configurations, customizations, or work product created specifically for Customer pursuant to a Statement of Work ('Deliverables') shall be owned by Customer upon full payment of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains ownership of its pre-existing materials, methodologies, and tools, and Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable license to use such pre-existing materials to the extent incorporated in the Deliverables.
external_comment: Section 2.5 does not specify ownership of Professional Services deliverables. We should own work product created specifically for us and paid for separately, while Lattice retains its pre-existing IP.
materiality_rationale: Customer pays separately for Professional Services that create custom configurations, integrations, or implementations specific to Customer's environment. Under the current silence combined with Section 6.1's broad sweep, Lattice could claim ownership of all such work product. This creates uncertainty about Customer's rights to use, modify, or migrate deliverables it has paid to create.
position: Customer owns deliverables created and paid for under SOWs; Lattice retains pre-existing materials and receives ability to reuse methodologies.
fallback: Joint ownership of deliverables with each party having unrestricted use rights; Lattice may reuse in anonymized/generalized form.
suggested_text: 2.5(a) Deliverables Ownership. Any deliverables, configurations, customizations, or work product created specifically for Customer pursuant to a Statement of Work ('Deliverables') shall be owned by Customer upon full payment of the applicable Fees. Lattice retains ownership of its pre-existing materials, methodologies, and tools, and Customer receives a perpetual, irrevocable license to use such pre-existing materials to the extent incorporated in the Deliverables.

proposed_text: For purposes of Section 4.3, a 'material breach' means a breach that substantially impairs the value of this Agreement to the non-breaching Party. Without limiting the foregoing, the following shall be deemed material breaches: (a) any breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions) or Section 7 (Confidentiality) by Customer; (b) any failure by Lattice to provide the Subscription Services in substantial conformance with the service-level commitments in Exhibit A for three (3) consecutive months; (c) any Security Incident that results in unauthorized access to or loss of Customer Data affecting more than 1,000 records; or (d) non-payment of undisputed Fees by Customer for more than thirty (30) days past due.
external_comment: The Agreement uses the term 'materially breaches' in Section 4.3 without defining 'material.' Adding a definition with illustrative examples provides both parties with clarity on what constitutes a termination-level breach, reducing the risk of disputes over whether a breach is sufficiently material to warrant termination.
materiality_rationale: Without a definition of 'material breach,' the parties face uncertainty and potential dispute over whether a given breach is sufficient to trigger termination rights. This ambiguity creates risk for both parties: Customer may be unable to terminate for a serious Lattice failure if Lattice argues it is not 'material,' and Lattice may face customer claims that minor infractions justify termination. The examples provided anchor the definition to objectively verifiable events that align with industry practice.
position: Insert a definition of 'material breach' with illustrative examples tied to objective thresholds.
suggested_text: For purposes of Section 4.3, a 'material breach' means a breach that substantially impairs the value of this Agreement to the non-breaching Party. Without limiting the foregoing, the following shall be deemed material breaches: (a) any breach of Section 2.2 (Restrictions) or Section 7 (Confidentiality) by Customer; (b) any failure by Lattice to provide the Subscription Services in substantial conformance with the service-level commitments in Exhibit A for three (3) consecutive months; (c) any Security Incident that results in unauthorized access to or loss of Customer Data affecting more than 1,000 records; or (d) non-payment of undisputed Fees by Customer for more than thirty (30) days past due.

source_text: Section 4.4(b): 'if Customer terminates this Agreement other than for Lattice's uncured material breach pursuant to Section 4.3, Customer shall pay all Fees that would have been payable for the remainder of the then-current Subscription Term'
external_comment: The accepted finding termination-remedies-analyst-003 proposes changing the arbitration venue from San Francisco to Customer's home state or mutually agreed neutral location. However, Section 4.4(b) creates a financial disincentive for Customer to pursue termination even when Lattice commits a material breach: if Customer terminates for Lattice's breach under Section 4.3 (with the newly proposed symmetric 30-day cure period from termination-remedies-analyst-002), Customer avoids paying the balance of the term — but if Lattice refuses to cure and the dispute goes to arbitration, the forum clause now favors Customer. This is internally consistent. However, we flag that the early-termination payment obligation in 4.4(b) should be reviewed to ensure it does not inadvertently create a walkaway cost that undermines the improved termination-for-breach mechanics. Specifically: if Customer must pay Fees through end of term when terminating other than for Lattice's uncured breach, but Lattice disputes whether its breach was 'material' or whether it was 'cured,' Customer faces litigation risk on the characterization before knowing whether it owes the balance. Consider adding: 'For avoidance of doubt, if a Party terminates pursuant to Section 4.3 and the other Party disputes whether the breach was material or uncured, the terminating Party's payment obligations under this Section 4.4(b) shall be stayed pending resolution of the dispute, and if the terminating Party prevails in establishing that termination was proper under Section 4.3, no Fees for the post-termination period shall be owed.'
materiality_rationale: The accepted termination-for-breach finding (termination-remedies-analyst-002) equalizes cure periods at 30 days, strengthening Customer's termination rights. But Section 4.4(b) creates an economic disincentive that could deter Customer from exercising those rights: even if Lattice commits a material breach (e.g., sustained SLA failure, Security Incident, failure to meet SOC 2 obligations), Customer must make a bet on whether Lattice's breach will be deemed 'material' and 'uncured' in arbitration before terminating. If Customer guesses wrong, it owes the full balance of the term. This payment obligation undermines the improved termination mechanics and creates concrete economic exposure in any borderline-materiality breach scenario. The proposed clarification ensures that the early-termination payment is stayed during dispute resolution, eliminating the chilling effect on Customer's exercise of its termination right.
position: Add clarifying language to Section 4.4(b) stating that if a Party terminates under Section 4.3 and the other Party disputes the termination, the terminating Party's obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the term is stayed pending resolution of the dispute. If the terminating Party prevails, no post-termination Fees are owed.
fallback: Add language requiring the disputing Party to post a bond or place disputed Fees in escrow during the dispute resolution process.

source_text: Section 2.3: 'Lattice's sole obligations and Customer's sole and exclusive remedies for failures to meet such service-level commitments are the service credits described in Exhibit A. Service credits do not constitute liquidated damages or limit Lattice's rights under this Agreement, and Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.'
external_comment: The accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes amending Section 2.3 to delete the sole-remedy language and add an express right to terminate without further payment obligation if SLA failures persist for three consecutive months. However, the existing Section 2.3 already states that 'Customer's sole remedy in the event of repeated failures shall be the right to terminate the affected Subscription Service in accordance with Section 4.3.' This creates internal tension: the existing language says termination under Section 4.3 is the remedy for repeated SLA failures, but Section 4.3 requires a 60-day cure period (or 30 days if termination-remedies-analyst-002 is accepted) and would trigger the early-termination payment obligation in Section 4.4(b) unless Lattice's SLA failure is deemed a 'material breach' that Lattice failed to cure. The accepted finding correctly identifies that SLA credits are inadequate as the sole remedy for sustained failures, but the proposed edit should also clarify how it interacts with the existing 'terminate in accordance with Section 4.3' language in Section 2.3. Recommend: replace the existing Section 2.3 termination language with the new three-consecutive-month termination right, and clarify that such termination does NOT require compliance with Section 4.3's cure-period mechanics and does NOT trigger Section 4.4(b)'s early-termination payment obligation.
materiality_rationale: The existing Section 2.3 says Customer can terminate for repeated SLA failures 'in accordance with Section 4.3,' which imposes a cure period and potentially the early-termination payment. The accepted finding performance-obligations-analyst-001 proposes a cleaner termination right (three consecutive months of SLA failure = terminate without further payment), but does not explicitly supersede the existing 'in accordance with Section 4.3' language. This creates interpretive ambiguity: does the three-month termination right operate independently of Section 4.3, or must Customer still give Lattice a cure notice under 4.3? If the latter, Lattice could argue that the three-month SLA failure triggers a cure period, during which Lattice has additional time to remediate before termination becomes effective — negating the intent of the proposed amendment. The coherence issue is that two termination paths for SLA failure now exist in the contract with unclear interaction. Clarifying language is needed to establish that the three-consecutive-month SLA termination right in Section 2.3 is independent of Section 4.3 and does not trigger Section 4.4(b)'s payment obligation.
position: Clarify in the amended Section 2.3 that the three-consecutive-month SLA termination right operates independently of Section 4.3's cure-period requirements and does not trigger Section 4.4(b)'s obligation to pay Fees for the remainder of the term.
fallback: Clarify that the three-month SLA termination right is 'in addition to' (not 'in accordance with') Section 4.3, and that termination under this provision is deemed termination for Lattice's material breach for purposes of Section 4.4(b).

source_text: Section 5.3: 'Lattice will notify Customer in writing without undue delay, and in any event within seventy-two (72) hours, after becoming aware of any unauthorized access to or acquisition of Customer Data' + Section 9.1: 'Lattice may engage third parties to perform any of its obligations under this Agreement, including without limitation hosting, content delivery, support, and processing of Customer Data. Lattice will remain responsible for the performance of any such subcontractors'
external_comment: The accepted finding critical-issues-auditor-002 proposes amending Section 9.1 to require advance notice and approval for subcontractors processing Customer Data, with mandatory flowdown of Sections 5 and 7. This is correct and necessary. However, Section 5.3's 72-hour breach notification obligation does not explicitly state that Lattice must notify Customer of Security Incidents caused by or discovered by subcontractors within the same 72-hour window. As drafted, if a subcontractor suffers a breach and delays reporting it to Lattice, the 72-hour clock may not start until Lattice 'becomes aware' — but Lattice's awareness is now one step removed from the breach. The amended Section 9.1 should clarify that for purposes of Section 5.3, Lattice is deemed to 'become aware' of a Security Incident at the same time any subcontractor processing Customer Data becomes aware, such that the 72-hour notification clock starts when the subcontractor knows (not when Lattice knows). This ensures the notification obligation is not diluted by the subcontracting chain.
materiality_rationale: Subcontractors processing Customer Data create supply-chain notification risk. If a subcontractor discovers a breach on Day 0 but delays reporting to Lattice until Day 5, and Lattice then notifies Customer 72 hours later (Day 8), Customer receives notification 8 days after the breach — well beyond regulatory safe-harbor windows in many U.S. state breach-notification laws. The accepted subcontracting amendment requires flowdown of Section 5 obligations, but does not explicitly address the 'becomes aware' trigger in Section 5.3. Clarifying that Lattice is deemed aware when any subcontractor becomes aware ensures the 72-hour clock is not gamed by delayed internal reporting within Lattice's supply chain.
position: Amend Section 5.3 or the proposed Section 9.1 amendment to clarify that for purposes of the 72-hour notification requirement, Lattice is deemed to 'become aware' of a Security Incident at the time any subcontractor processing Customer Data becomes aware of the incident.
fallback: Require subcontractors to notify Lattice within 24 hours of becoming aware of a Security Incident, and require Lattice to notify Customer within 48 hours of receiving such notice from a subcontractor (net 72-hour maximum from subcontractor awareness to Customer notification).
jurisdiction_assumed: Delaware (Section 10.1); U.S. state breach-notification laws impose strict timelines regardless of contractual notification chains.
